summary: Evans argues that despite machine learning being powerful and exciting, people should maintain healthy skepticism about its outputs. She identifies three key reasons for skepticism: models are limited by their training data and its biases, ML programs can contain ordinary software bugs that get mistaken for sophisticated behavior, and even well-built models frequently produce wrong results. She uses relatable examples like predicting nationality from names and Google Photos misclassifying images to illustrate these points. The post encourages non-specialists to question ML results rather than deferring to the technology's perceived authority.
key_points: Machine learning models inherit the biases and gaps of their training data, so underrepresented populations get poor results
ML programs are still software and have regular bugs, which people sometimes excuse as the model being 'cool and smart'
Even bug-free models trained on good data will produce confidently wrong individual predictions
Non-specialists questioning ML outputs are often right that something is wrong, and their skepticism should be encouraged
ML systems can appear objective but are not immune to producing absurd or biased results
money_quotes: machine learning models are only as good as the data you put into them
if you don't include any data from China when training your computer to recognize names, it's not going to get any Chinese names right!

someone's said to me 'hey julia, why did the machine learning make this decision? it seems wrong?' and I've said 'oh, you know, machine learning! it's probably doing something cool and smart'. But then I look into it anyway. And then, rather than something cool and smart, the program I work on just had a bug.
I was really happy that the people asking the questions didn't assume that the machine learning was Correct and Smart, but that instead told us 'hey, this looks wrong'
just because something came out of a statistical model it isn't 'objective' or even necessarily correct
Machine learning is super powerful and you can build models that do amazing things, but they can also tell you that a donald trump pinata is a picture of a baby, and that makes no sense.
key_insight: Machine learning outputs deserve the same skepticism as any other software output — they can reflect biased data, contain bugs, or simply be wrong, and non-experts questioning results are often right to do so.
